articles.title: The Heist That Made the Mona Lisa Famous: Andrew Lloyd Webber's Next Musical
articles.summary: Think the Mona Lisa has always been the world's most famous painting? Think again. Before 1911, it was just one of many masterpieces at the Louvre. Then it vanished. Broadway legend Andrew Lloyd Webber is now writing a musical about the bizarre true story of how an Italian glazier named Vincenzo Peruggia stole the painting and hid it in his trunk for two years. The theft turned the Mona Lisa into a global sensation. Lloyd Webber promises spectacle, of course. Get ready for a heist with songs!

articles.title: The Photographer Who Finds Warmth in Black and White: Austn Fischer's Art of Contrast
articles.summary: Austn Fischer's black-and-white photography is anything but cold. The Wisconsin-born, London-based artist uses contrast in surprising ways — pairing lace ruffs with athletic gear, or a sharp black gown with a delicate horse-shaped wire sculpture. He says photography helps him understand emotions he can't put into words. Fischer has shot famous subjects like Ai Weiwei and David Byrne for major magazines. Recently, he spent two weeks painting a church wall white to learn patience. His work is a warm, playful exploration of identity.

articles.title: Resurrected from Ruins: A Bamiyan Buddha Made from War Metal Now Stands on the High Line
articles.summary: A 27-foot-tall sandstone Buddha has appeared on New York City's High Line, but it's far more than a sculpture. It's a powerful recreation of the ancient Bamiyan Buddhas destroyed by the Taliban in 2001. Artist Tuan Andrew Nguyen made the statue's hands from melted-down artillery sourced from Afghanistan — transforming weapons of war into symbols of peace. The right hand forms the Abhaya mudra (fearlessness), the left the Varada mudra (compassion). Titled 'The Light That Shines Through the Universe,' the statue is on view through spring 2027.
